name: Modified N-free Hoagland solution + 20 mM glucose, no iron plaque (control, CK) (PMC13134045)
description: Modified N-free Hoagland solution + 20 mM glucose, no iron plaque (control, CK) — medium reported in PMC13134045, extracted for GrowthDB and mapped to BiGG.

provenance.citation: Medium composition from PMC13134045 (see GrowthDB record).
provenance.notes: Composition mapped to BiGG (mapper + salt dissociation); presence-based bounds; paper_amount retained. Added via GrowthDB curation.
provenance.formulation_warning: workflow correction sparse; kept original — This is the N-FREE control (CK) microcosm medium. Current formulation is wrong on three counts: (1) it lists Nitrate and Ammonium, but the paper explicitly REMOVES all nitrogen (N-free) by swapping Ca(NO3)2, KNO3, NH4H2PO4 for CaCl2.2H2O, KH2PO4, K2SO4 - so NO3 and NH4 must be deleted; (2) it lists O2 and marks oxygen 'facultative', but the microcosm is deoxygenated with N2, sealed anoxic, and the organism (Clostridium saccharoperbutylacetonicum) is a strict anaerobe - oxygen is anaerobic and O2 must be removed; (3) it omits the Mg and the micronutrient set (B, Mn, Zn, Cu, Mo, Fe) that belong to the standard Hoagland base. The paper does not tabulate the Hoagland salt/micronutrient concentrations, so amounts below are the standard full-strength Hoagland base values (medium confidence); the N-salt substitutions and glucose are directly from the paper. Fe (Fe-EDTA) is a standard Hoagland micronutrient and is included, but given the study's tight control of iron availability its presence/level in this control is not confirmed in the main text - flagged as uncertain.
provenance.verification_evidence: 20 ml of modified N-free Hoagland solution (pH 6.0). Nitrogen was omitted by replacing all N-containing salts (Ca(NO3)2.4H2O, KNO3, and NH4H2PO4) with equimolar amounts of their chloride- or sulfate-based counterparts (CaCl2.2H2O, KH2PO4, and K2SO4)... deoxygenated with N2, sealed... filter-sterilized glucose was added... to a final concentration of 20 mM.
